title: What levers do you pull when LLM output quality drops, and why is retraining rarely the first move?
prompt:
What levers do you pull when LLM output quality drops, and why is retraining rarely the first move?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
In classical ML the answer is often "get more data and retrain." In LLMOps, retraining is the last resort — leverage favors faster, cheaper interventions, roughly in this order: (1) Prompt engineering — fixes task rules and ambiguity, takes hours, deploys instantly. (2) Retrieval improvement — better chunking and embeddings fix what looks like "model quality" bugs. (3) Few-shot examples — adding selected examples fixes specific failure patterns cheaply. (4) Model switch — moving to a more capable model addresses genuine capability gaps. (5) Fine-tuning — justified only with hundreds of labeled failure examples and a stable behavior change; expensive, slow, and creates a new artifact to maintain.

**Takeaway:** Most teams using external APIs don't even have access to retrain base models, and fine-tuning's time investment is only justified after cheaper options are exhausted — always work down the leverage ladder before reaching for retraining.
hint_notes: Order of cheapest-to-most-expensive fix: prompt eng → retrieval → few-shot → model switch → fine-tuning. Retraining/fine-tuning is the last resort, not the default.
